---
title: "HONR 46400: Evidence-Driven Research"
subtitle: "Honors College, Purdue University (Fall 2026 — Mon/Wed/Fri)"
---

<br>

<center>

*IMPORTANT*

*This document does not replace the official material in the course Brightspace page*

</center>

<br>

## Course Description and Objectives

<!-- Duplicated in syllabus.qmd — keep the two in sync on every edit. -->

This Honors seminar teaches you to design, analyze, verify, and defend your own empirical research, with **no strong background in quantitative methods or computing required**. Over one semester you will carry a single project from curiosity to a publicly defended claim. You will find a real gap in a field you care about, scope it into a researchable problem, and ask answerable questions. You will classify each question on the **inquiry compass** (its kind, descriptive vs causal, and its reach, the data at hand vs a population beyond it vs cases not yet seen) and match it to a **design** from the research library (observational or experimental, descriptive or causal, with prediction as its own objective). You will declare that design, diagnose it by simulation, redesign it, gather and verify evidence, attack your own analysis, and communicate a claim with its uncertainty and limitations in **written and oral** form.

Throughout, you will use AI at high intensity and under tight control. The course's defining message is simple:

> **AI is your arm and your research assistant — not your brain.**
> AI may propose. The researcher must verify. The evidence must support.

**Course Website**: [https://davi-moreira.github.io/2026F_evidence_driven_research_purdue_HONR464/](https://davi-moreira.github.io/2026F_evidence_driven_research_purdue_HONR464/){target="_blank"}

### Instructor: [Professor Davi Moreira](https://davi-moreira.github.io)

-   Email: [dcordeir\@purdue.edu](mailto:dcordeir@purdue.edu)
-   Class meetings: **Monday / Wednesday / Friday** — in person (time and room on Brightspace)
-   Office Hours: *See the syllabus on your Course Brightspace Page, or by appointment.*

## Learning Outcomes

<!-- Duplicated in syllabus.qmd — keep in sync. -->

By the conclusion of this course, you will be able to:

1.  **Find a gap and scope a problem.** Locate a meaningful gap in a field you care about and turn it into a well-scoped, researchable problem.

2.  **Ask answerable questions and classify them on the inquiry compass.** Turn a problem into questions evidence can address, and place each on the compass by kind (descriptive vs causal) and reach (data at hand, a population beyond it, or cases not yet seen), justifying the classification and its limits without heavy mathematics.

3.  **Declare a research design on the library pathways.** Match a classified question to a design (observational or experimental, descriptive or causal, with prediction treated as its own objective) and declare it with the **MIDA** framework: model, inquiry, data strategy, answer strategy.

4.  **Diagnose and redesign before collecting data.** Simulate a declared design, read its diagnosis, and redesign it so it can actually answer the question.

5.  **Gather and analyze evidence responsibly with AI.** Direct AI tools to locate sources, operationalize concepts, and analyze evidence, and **verify** every result and citation against real, retrievable evidence while documenting each decision.

6.  **Attack your own analysis.** Stress-test a finding with robustness checks and adversarial red-teaming, and reproduce a peer's package in a replication and red-team review.

7.  **Communicate under uncertainty, in writing and out loud.** Present a claim with its boundary, uncertainty, and limitations as a poster at the Purdue Fall Undergraduate Research Expo, a written research note, and a public evidence defense.

8.  **Manage AI as a directed research assistant.** Run the **Specify, Delegate, Interrogate, Inspect, Verify, Document, Defend** workflow, coordinate multiple AI roles, and keep a complete **AI Research Ledger**, because AI is your arm and your research assistant, not your brain.

## Course Materials

-   **Primary text (free):** The course's primary text is **Blair, Coppock & Humphreys (2023), *Research Design in the Social Sciences: Declaration, Diagnosis, and Redesign*** (Princeton University Press) — read **free online** at [book.declaredesign.org](https://book.declaredesign.org/){target="_blank"}; no purchase required. Chapters are read conceptually (no quantitative prerequisite), on the light cadence in the course schedule.

-   **Course book (free):** the course's own open text, **_Evidence-Driven Research: How to Design, Analyze, Verify, and Defend Empirical Research_**, published on the course website. It walks through the same material at an honors-undergraduate reading level and links each chapter to its Colab lab.

-   **Optional companion:** Bergstrom & West, *Calling Bullshit* (public case studies at [callingbullshit.org](https://callingbullshit.org/){target="_blank"}). Any additional readings are posted on Brightspace.

-   **Computing (Required):** A laptop or desktop with internet access and a modern web browser. Course activities run in the browser through [Google Colab](https://colab.research.google.com/){target="_blank"} — no local installation required. There are **no purchases** required for this course.

-   **AI Tools:**

    -   [**Google Gemini**](https://gemini.google.com/){target="_blank"} is the **primary** research assistant, used in and alongside Colab under the course's **Ask → Verify → Document** policy: draft with AI assistance, verify every claim and source against real, retrievable evidence, and document your decisions. You own every claim you submit.
    -   [**Purdue GenAI Studio**](https://genai.rcac.purdue.edu){target="_blank"} supplies custom **reviewer roles** you are required to consult at four milestones (M5, M7, M9, M13).
    -   [**Microsoft Copilot**](https://it.purdue.edu/newsroom/2024/240313-copilot-with-data-protection.php){target="_blank"} (Purdue-provided, with data protection) and other general-purpose tools are permitted with disclosure.

## Course Infra-structure

**Brightspace:** The Course Brightspace Page <https://purdue.brightspace.com/> should be checked on a regular basis for announcements and course material.
